section.title: The Two Beasts
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is the dragon's answer to his own defeat in chapter 12. Unable to destroy the woman and her offspring directly, he raises up two proxies: a beast from the sea who wields blasphemous imperial power, and a beast from the earth who compels worship of the first beast through deceptive signs and economic coercion. The unit holds together as the full unveiling of the dragon's unholy counterfeit trinity, dragon, beast, and false prophet, before the vision of the Lamb and His people in chapter 14 directly answers it.
section.thesis: The dragon rules through two beasts, one that wields blasphemous political power and wins the world's worship by imitating a resurrection, and one that enforces that worship through deceptive signs and economic coercion, so that every person, marked or unmarked, is forced to choose whom they will serve.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the beast from the sea and its blasphemous authority; the beast's war on the saints and the call to perseverance; the beast from the earth, the false prophet who deceives with signs; and the mark of the beast, its name, and the number 666.

verses.text: Then I saw a {{therion|beast}} with ten horns and seven heads rising out of the sea. There were ten royal crowns on its horns and blasphemous names on its heads.
The beast I saw was like a leopard, with the feet of a bear and the mouth of a lion. And the dragon gave the beast his power and his throne and great authority.
One of the heads of the beast appeared to be mortally wounded. But the mortal wound was healed, and the whole world marveled and followed the beast.
They {{proskyneo|worshiped}} the dragon who had given authority to the beast, and they worshiped the beast, saying, “Who is like the beast, and who can wage war against it?”

units.label: War on the saints, and the call to endure
units.note: a limited authority, an unlimited demand

verses.text: The beast was given a mouth to speak arrogant and blasphemous words, and authority to act for 42 months.
And the beast opened its mouth to speak blasphemies against God and to slander His name and His tabernacle, those who dwell in heaven.
Then the beast was permitted to wage war against the saints and to conquer them, and it was given authority over every tribe and people and tongue and nation.
And all who dwell on the earth will worship the beast, all whose names have not been written from the foundation of the world in the Book of Life belonging to the Lamb who was slain.
He who has an ear, let him hear:

“If anyone is destined for captivity, into captivity he will go; if anyone is to die by the sword, by the sword he must be killed.” Here is a call for the {{hypomone|perseverance}} and faith of the saints.

units.label: The beast from the earth
units.note: the false prophet who deceives with signs

verses.text: Then I saw another beast rising out of the earth. This beast had two horns like a lamb, but spoke like a dragon.
And this beast exercised all the authority of the first beast and caused the earth and those who dwell in it to worship the first beast, whose mortal wound had been healed.
And the second beast performed great signs to cause even fire from heaven to come down to earth in the presence of the people.

Because of the signs it was given to perform on behalf of the first beast, it deceived those who dwell on the earth, telling them to make an image to the beast that had been wounded by the sword and yet had lived.
The second beast was permitted to give breath to the image of the first beast, so that the image could speak and cause all who refused to worship it to be killed.

units.label: The mark, the name, and the number
units.note: economic coercion and a call for wisdom

verses.text: And the second beast required all people small and great, rich and poor, free and slave, to receive a {{charagma|mark}} on their right hand or on their forehead,
so that no one could buy or sell unless he had the mark, the name of the beast or the number of its name.
Here is a call for wisdom: Let the one who has insight calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and that number is 666.

therion.gloss: A wild beast, a monster. John draws its features, leopard, bear, and lion, straight from Daniel's four beasts, now combined into one final, composite empire. The word marks the beast as an animal in nature, however human its face, a creature of appetite and violence set against the Lamb who was slain.

proskyneo.gloss: To worship, to fall down and pay homage. It is the same word used throughout Revelation for the worship owed to God and the Lamb alone; here the whole world bows it to the dragon and the beast instead, the counterfeit of true worship at the heart of the chapter.

charagma.gloss: A mark, stamp, or engraved brand, used of the imperial seal on official documents and of brands on slaves or soldiers. The beast's charagma on hand and forehead parodies the seal of the living God placed on His servants' foreheads in chapter 7, forcing visible allegiance into the ordinary business of daily life.

hypomone.gloss: Steadfast endurance, perseverance under pressure rather than passive waiting. It is the quality repeatedly called for as the saints face the beast's authority to conquer them; it does not mean escaping suffering but remaining faithful through it, after the pattern of the Lamb who was slain.

basic.context: Chapter 12 ended with the dragon defeated in heaven but furious on earth. Now he raises two agents: a beast from the sea that combines the horror of Daniel's four beasts and wields the dragon's own power, and a beast from the earth that works miracles to force the world to worship the first beast. Together they demand worship and control commerce through a mark, so that no one can buy or sell without declaring allegiance.
basic.hermeneutics: Readers debate exactly who or what the beast and its number, 666, pointed to first, whether a Roman emperor like Nero or Domitian, a future end-time ruler, or a recurring pattern of God-defying power in every age. The chapter itself does not require solving that puzzle to be understood: it is meant to unmask worldly power and false religion as the dragon's instruments and to call suffering believers to discernment and perseverance rather than compromise.
basic.originalLanguages: The therion, the beast, is an animal at heart however impressive its crowns. The world offers it proskyneo, worship, that belongs to God alone. It brands its followers with a charagma, a mark, on hand or forehead, a counterfeit of God's own seal. And in the face of its authority, the saints are called to hypomone, steady endurance, and faith.
basic.typology: The beast's healed mortal wound apes the true death and resurrection of the Lamb, a counterfeit miracle for a counterfeit king. Daniel's four beasts, lion, bear, leopard, and a terrifying fourth, are gathered into this one final beast, showing that every proud empire in history was a preview of this last, blasphemous power.
basic.christ: Against the beast's fake resurrection stands the Lamb who was truly slain and truly lives, and it is His Book of Life, not the beast's mark, that secures a person's name. The call to endurance echoes Christ's own patient suffering, and it is by trusting Him, not by military resistance, that the saints are told to face the beast.
basic.god: God's sovereignty frames the whole chapter: the beast is repeatedly said to be given, permitted, allowed to act, and for a limited time, 42 months, not forever. However fierce the beast, it operates only within boundaries God Himself sets, and the outcome is already decided in the Book of Life kept from the foundation of the world.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7:2-7 supplies the beast's composite features. Revelation 7:2-3 shows God's own seal on His servants' foreheads, the pattern the beast's mark counterfeits. Revelation 12:9 and 17 explain the dragon's fury behind both beasts. And Revelation 20:10 shows the beast and false prophet finally and fully judged.
basic.oneStory: From the serpent in Eden to the beasts of Daniel to this final beast, one enemy keeps raising counterfeit power to demand the worship due to God alone. The Bible's answer is consistent: the slain and risen Lamb, whose Book of Life secures His people against every empire, mark, and counterfeit miracle the dragon can produce.

context.literary: Chapter 13 continues directly from the dragon's defeat and fury in chapter 12; having failed to destroy the woman and her offspring, he stations himself on the seashore and calls up two beasts as his instruments. The unit is answered point for point by chapter 14, where the Lamb stands with His own marked people and three angels proclaim the true gospel against the beast's counterfeit worship.
context.historical: Written to churches in Roman Asia under real pressure to honor the imperial cult, where refusing to burn incense to Caesar or to participate in trade guild rituals tied to pagan gods could mean social exclusion, economic hardship, or death, as already seen in the martyrdom of Antipas at Pergamum, Revelation 2:13.
context.canonical: The beast draws together the four beasts of Daniel 7 into one final, composite empire, and its persecution of the saints anticipates the beast's own destruction in Revelation 19 and 20. The mark of the beast stands as the deliberate counterfeit of the seal of God placed on His servants in Revelation 7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To unmask worldly power and false religion as the dragon's instruments, so that suffering believers would refuse to worship the beast even at the cost of life, trusting the Lamb who has already conquered and whose Book of Life secures them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the character and strategy of the dragon's proxies across history rather than handing readers a chronological key to decode current events. Its abiding, prescriptive force is discernment of every power and religious system that demands ultimate allegiance, and steadfast endurance in refusing to give it.

debates.question: Who or what is the beast from the sea, and what does 666 mean?

views.label: A first-century emperor, Nero or Domitian, and the cult of Rome
views.case: Read in its own setting, the beast combines Daniel's four beasts into the Roman empire, whose emperors demanded divine honors. The healed mortal wound may recall the legend that Nero would return from death, and in Hebrew gematria the letters of 'Nero Caesar' sum to 666, with some early manuscripts reading 616, matching a Latinized spelling of the same name, so John's first hearers could plausibly have recognized their own persecutor.

views.label: A future end-time ruler, or a timeless pattern in every beastly regime
views.case: Because the vision's universal worship, economic control, and warfare against the saints outstrip anything Nero or Domitian actually achieved, many read the beast as a still-future individual who will revive and perfect the dragon's programme, while others read it as a recurring pattern embodied differently by every generation's God-defying power, with 666 marking a persistent, threefold falling short of God's completeness, pictured by the number seven.

debates.bearing: Whichever referent is primarily in view, the passage's force does not depend on solving the puzzle. The beast is real, blasphemous, and demands what belongs to God alone, and every generation must discern its counterfeit worship and refuse it.

debates.question: Is the mark of the beast a specific, literal mark, or a symbol of total allegiance?

views.label: A literal, identifiable mark
views.case: The text is specific about hand and forehead and about buying and selling, paralleling real ancient practices of stamped certificates for trade and branding of soldiers and slaves, so many futurist readers expect a literal, identifiable mark still to come, perhaps tied to future technology.

views.label: A symbol of total economic and religious conformity
views.case: The mark parodies God's own seal placed on the foreheads of His servants in chapter 7, so the point is allegiance made visible in the ordinary transactions of daily life rather than one specific technology. On this reading the mark names whatever system forces a person's livelihood into open allegiance to the beast.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the mark represents a real, costly choice between visible allegiance to the beast's system and joining the Lamb's marked, persevering people, whatever concrete form that choice takes in a given generation.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's force lies in a handful of loaded words. The therion, the beast, is an animal, however crowned and however human-sounding its blasphemies. The world gives it proskyneo, the worship due to God alone, deceived by its counterfeit resurrection. It presses a charagma, a mark, into hand and forehead, a forced brand that parodies God's own seal on His people. And to the saints under its authority, John holds out one word above all others: hypomone, steadfast endurance, holding to faith even when the beast is permitted, for a time, to conquer them.

typology.fulfillment: A satanic counterfeit of the true death and resurrection of the Lamb, exposing every claim to power that imitates Christ's victory without His cross.

typology.type: Daniel's four beasts combined into one
typology.fulfillment: Every proud empire of history gathered into a single, final, composite power, judged and destroyed at Christ's return, Daniel 7:2-7 and Revelation 19:20.

typology.type: The beast's mark on hand and forehead
typology.fulfillment: The counterfeit of God's own seal on His servants, Revelation 7:2-3, contrasting two competing marks of ultimate ownership and allegiance.

study.christ: Everything the beast fakes, Christ actually did. Its healed mortal wound imitates a resurrection it cannot truly produce; the Lamb was truly slain and truly lives, and His name, not the beast's, is written on the foreheads of His own. It is the Book of Life belonging to the Lamb who was slain, kept from the foundation of the world, that decides who worships the beast and who does not. And the call to the saints under the beast's temporary authority to conquer them is a call to follow the Lamb's own pattern: faithful endurance through suffering rather than escape from it, trusting that His victory, already accomplished, secures theirs.
study.god: God's sovereignty governs even the dragon's fiercest instruments. The beast's mouth, its authority, its permission to make war on the saints, its 42 months, all are described as given or permitted, never as beyond God's control. He alone holds the Book of Life from before the foundation of the world, and no beast, however blasphemous its claims, can add or remove a name from it. His restraint of evil for an appointed, limited time is itself an act of sovereign mercy toward His suffering people.

crossReferences.note: The lion, bear, leopard, and terrifying fourth beast that John gathers into this one final, composite beast.
God's own seal placed on the foreheads of His servants, the pattern the beast's mark deliberately counterfeits.
The dragon's defeat in heaven and his resulting fury against the woman's offspring, the setting that produces both beasts.
The beast and the false prophet finally and fully judged, the certain end of the powers described here.

study.oneStory: From the serpent's first lie in Eden, through the beasts that rose against Daniel's people, to this final beast from the sea and its false prophet from the earth, one enemy keeps raising counterfeit power to demand the worship that belongs to God alone. Every time, the Bible's answer is the same: a slain and risen Lamb whose Book of Life secures His people against every empire, every mark, and every counterfeit miracle the dragon can produce, calling them not to fight the beast on its own terms but to endure in faith until He returns to destroy it.

sermon.title: The Beast, the Book, and the Beauty of the Lamb
sermon.why: Piper expounds Revelation 13:1-10 directly, centering on the Book of Life of the slain Lamb as the reason believers will not worship the beast, which is exactly the turn this study makes from the beast's counterfeit power to the security found only in Christ.

quiz.q: What does the beast from the sea receive from the dragon?

options.text: The dragon's own power, throne, and great authority.
options.note: Revelation 13:2 states the dragon gave the beast his power, throne, and authority.

options.text: Only a temporary alliance with no shared authority.
options.note: The text is stronger than an alliance; the dragon directly transfers his power and throne.

options.text: A crown taken from the Lamb.
options.note: The beast's authority comes from the dragon, not from the Lamb.

options.text: Nothing; the beast acts entirely on its own.
options.note: The beast is explicitly empowered by the dragon, not self-sufficient.

quiz.q: Whose names are protected from worshiping the beast?

options.text: Those written from the foundation of the world in the Lamb's Book of Life.
options.note: Revelation 13:8 names this as the deciding factor.

options.text: Anyone who is politically powerful enough to resist.
options.note: The protection is not political strength but being written in the Lamb's book.

options.text: Only the twelve apostles.
options.note: The promise is for all whose names are in the Book of Life, not a fixed small group.

options.text: No one; all people without exception worship the beast.
options.note: The text explicitly excepts those written in the Book of Life.

quiz.q: What is the role of the second beast, from the earth?

options.text: It performs deceptive signs and forces worship of the first beast, including through an image that speaks.
options.note: Revelation 13:12-15 describes this false-prophet role.

options.text: It replaces the first beast as the main ruler.
options.note: It serves and promotes the first beast rather than replacing it.

options.text: It fights against the first beast to protect the saints.
options.note: It works for the first beast, enforcing worship of it, not opposing it.

options.text: It has no connection to the first beast.
options.note: It exists specifically to exercise the first beast's authority and compel its worship.

quiz.q: What does the mark of the beast control?

options.text: The ability to buy or sell in the ordinary business of daily life.
options.note: Revelation 13:17 ties the mark directly to commerce.

options.text: Only entrance into pagan temples.
options.note: The text specifies buying and selling, a broader, everyday reach than temple access.

options.text: Access to military service only.
options.note: The mark's stated purpose is economic, not military.

options.text: Nothing of practical consequence.
options.note: The mark has real, everyday economic consequences for those without it.

quiz.q: What response does the chapter call for in the face of the beast's authority?

options.text: Perseverance and faith, discerned with wisdom, not violent resistance.
options.note: Revelation 13:10 and 18 call for endurance, faith, and wisdom.

options.text: Immediate armed rebellion against the beast's forces.
options.note: The call is to endurance and faith, not armed revolt.

options.text: Silent agreement with whatever the beast demands.
options.note: The saints are called to refuse the beast's worship, not comply silently.

options.text: No response is called for at all.
options.note: The chapter explicitly calls for perseverance, faith, and wisdom.

questions: The beast controlled who could buy or sell. Where do you feel pressure today to compromise your convictions for the sake of financial or social security?
The whole world marveled at the beast's healed wound and followed it. What counterfeit forms of power or salvation are tempting to admire and follow in your own life right now?

The saints were called to perseverance and faith rather than fear or fighting on the world's terms. What would it look like this week to endure faithfully in a hard situation rather than escape it or compromise?
Only those written in the Lamb's Book of Life were kept from worshiping the beast. How does resting in that security, rather than in your own strength, change the way you face pressure to conform?

The chapter calls for wisdom to discern the beast's true nature rather than being deceived by its signs. Where do you need to ask God for discernment about a power, system, or trend that claims more allegiance than it deserves?

translationNotes.note: The 42 months is the same limited span found elsewhere in Revelation as 1,260 days or a time, times, and half a time, echoing Daniel 7:25 and 12:7; it marks a real but God-limited period of persecution, not an unbounded reign.

The Greek word order allows 'from the foundation of the world' to modify either 'written' (names written down from the foundation of the world) or 'slain' (the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world, as in some translations of Revelation 13:8 and the parallel in 17:8). Both readings are true to the rest of Scripture, and translators differ.

Some early manuscripts, including Papyrus 115, read 616 instead of 666. Both numbers have been connected historically to the gematria value of 'Nero Caesar' spelled different ways, though the text itself calls it simply 'the number of a man.'
The beast's composite features, a leopard's body, a bear's feet, and a lion's mouth, are drawn directly from the four separate beasts of Daniel 7:4-6, now combined into a single final power.

meta.notes: BSB verse text supplied pre-verified this session; verse 17's original em dash had already been converted to a comma before this text was provided, and no further em or en dashes were introduced. Straight quotation marks and apostrophes in the supplied text were rendered as curly quotation marks to match this repo's typographic convention; no wording was altered.
